items.q: What is the difference between CAD and CAM?
items.correct: CAD creates digital designs; CAM uses those designs to control manufacturing machines.
items.misconception: They are the same thing with different names.
items.random_wrong: CAD is for cars, CAM is for cameras.
items.why: Students treat the terms as synonymous because they often appear together. CAD = drawing/modelling software; CAM = machine control (CNC routers, 3D printers) - typically the second stage.

items.q: What does 'Rethink' mean in the 6 Rs?
items.correct: Questioning whether a product is necessary or could be designed differently for sustainability.
items.misconception: Trying a design again if it does not work first time.
items.why: Students apply 'rethink' to design iteration. In sustainability, it means challenging the NEED for a product altogether, or fundamentally rethinking how it's made - a more radical step than reducing materials.

items.correct: Designing products usable by the widest possible range of people without special adaptation.
items.random_wrong: Adding multiple language options to packaging.
items.why: Students conflate inclusive with specialist design. Inclusive design widens MAINSTREAM products to work for more people - the OXO Good Grips kitchen tools, designed for arthritic hands, work better for everyone.

items.q: Whose 2005 Double Diamond models the design process?
items.why: It's UK Design Council. “IDEO” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which technique systematically alters product attributes?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: SCAMPER. “TRIZ” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many diamonds are in the Double Diamond model?
items.why: Exact answer: 2 diamonds. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
